agent1_role: Dr. Elena Marquez, Head of the Archaeology Department at Northshore University
agent2_role: Dr. Simon Patel, Senior Research Fellow, Archaeology Department at Northshore University
generic:
Northshore University has built a strong reputation as a leading institution for interdisciplinary research and field-based study programs. To expand its reach and boost global recognition, the university has, over the past six years, invested heavily in acquiring excavation rights, digitizing collections, and forging partnerships with cultural institutions abroad. This approach has allowed the Archaeology Department, in particular, to secure consistent grant funding and attract both graduate students and international collaborators.  

The Archaeology Department is housed within the College of Arts and Sciences. It is overseen by a department head and supported by a group of senior and junior fellows specializing in different aspects of research, teaching, and fieldwork. The department manages four major activity areas: Excavation Projects, Archival and Collections Management, Digital Humanities, and Community Engagement. Each of these areas is expected to advance scholarship while contributing to the department’s visibility and sustainability.  

A central challenge for the department head is balancing the demands of field projects, academic publications, and teaching with the practical realities of team dynamics.  
Because many of the department’s projects arise from collaborations with external institutions or funders, maintaining smooth working relationships among staff members is essential. Senior fellows are often given great autonomy in managing grants, supervising graduate students, and coordinating with partner organizations. However, this independence can also create tensions when individuals prioritize their own projects at the expense of departmental needs. The head of department is frequently left to manage conflicts, particularly when scholars have longstanding personal or professional ties that complicate objective decision-making.  

Although the university has a central Office of Academic Personnel, its presence in departmental affairs is minimal. The office primarily handles contracts, formal evaluations, and compliance paperwork; it does not intervene in day-to-day management or disputes unless matters escalate significantly. As such, when disagreements arise within the Archaeology Department, especially among senior staff, it is typically the department head who must confront the issue directly.  

Recently, growing strain has surfaced in the Excavation Projects unit, based at Northshore’s Boston campus. The two key figures there are Department Head Dr. Elena Marquez and Senior Research Fellow Dr. Simon Patel. These two scholars share a long professional history. Over the past two years, the unit has also navigated the underperformance of a mid-career lateral hire championed by Dr. Marquez—a researcher whose background in cultural tourism proved ill-suited to the demands of active excavation work. This hire has added strain to team dynamics and created disagreement about where responsibility for the unit’s difficulties actually lies.
agent1_specific:
If someone had told you five years ago that you might now be considering ending your professional relationship with Simon Patel, you would have dismissed the idea outright. Yet, that difficult possibility is exactly what has been occupying your thoughts over the past several weeks. You have worked closely with Simon for more than twelve years. You first recruited him after his doctoral studies, when he impressed you with both his theoretical sophistication and his skill in managing field logistics during one of your Mediterranean excavation projects. At that time, you had just been appointed Head of the Archaeology Department at Northshore University, and one of your goals was to build a strong team of promising researchers who could complement your vision for collaborative, field-driven scholarship.  

Simon’s trajectory was remarkable. Early on, when you brought him onto small survey projects, he quickly earned a reputation for being meticulous in recording data and adept at building rapport with graduate students. He became your most trusted collaborator, acting as the operational anchor for projects whenever you needed to travel, lecture, or engage with funding agencies. Over time, his presence allowed you to expand the department’s portfolio of excavations and to deepen ties with international partners.  
Things began to shift about two years ago, not long after Simon married Leila, a conservation architect who soon launched her own small design consultancy specializing in heritage site preservation. While you initially celebrated their union and appreciated the thematic overlap with the department’s own work in cultural heritage, you began noticing subtle but important changes in Simon’s work habits. He has increasingly seemed to devote significant time and attention to helping Leila’s consultancy get off the ground, often taking calls during department hours or disappearing for part of the day under the pretense of “off-site errands.” At first, you were tolerant, reasoning that personal obligations sometimes demanded flexibility, and you trusted that he would keep his core responsibilities intact. Unfortunately, several instances have since tested your patience.  

For example, during last summer’s excavation season in Italy, you relied on Simon to coordinate site permits and liaise with local heritage authorities. Instead, you discovered that deadlines had been missed, jeopardizing both your fieldwork and a major grant tied to community engagement outcomes. You ended up stepping in at the last minute, leveraging personal contacts to prevent the permit from falling through. Simon brushed off the matter, apologizing but citing his exhaustion from juggling departmental demands with supporting Leila’s new business.  
What troubles you is not simply the occasional missed deadline, but Simon’s overall shift in priorities and attitude. In the past, he was eager to brainstorm strategies for expanding the department’s global profile, often staying late to refine grant applications or to mentor graduate students on their dissertations. Now, he seems more focused on the logistics of Leila’s consultancy than on proposing new initiatives for Northshore. His enthusiasm has waned; in meetings, he often appears distracted, offering cursory feedback or deferring responsibilities to others. Even when he produces solid work, it often lacks the forward-looking spark that once set him apart.  

A particular moment crystallized your frustration. Three months ago, you asked Simon to prepare a detailed progress update for the National Endowment for the Humanities, a critical document tied to multi-year funding for the department’s digital heritage program. Instead of delivering the report with his usual clarity and precision, Simon turned it in incomplete—so incomplete, in fact, that you had to rewrite large sections before submission. While he later defended himself by claiming overcommitment and fatigue, you are left wondering whether his loyalties are divided in ways that the department can no longer afford.  
At first, you tried to adapt to Simon’s shifting availability by granting him more autonomy and hoping he would self-correct. However, instead of stabilizing, the situation has slipped further. This became especially apparent during the autumn term, when you needed him to supervise a cohort of graduate students in the field school associated with the Boston Harbor excavation. Normally, this would have been a perfect assignment for him – mentoring students, overseeing meticulous data collection, and ensuring compliance with regulatory frameworks. Yet, several students complained privately that Simon was often absent on crucial days, leaving them uncertain about methodology and project goals. You had to field their questions yourself, on top of your own heavy teaching and grant-writing obligations.  

What stings the most is that Simon is still capable of delivering excellent results when he applies himself, making his lapses more frustrating. For instance, he produced a compelling report for a consortium of partner museums earlier this year, which won praise from external collaborators. But those flashes of brilliance feel increasingly rare, overshadowed by inconsistency and questionable priorities. You have begun to ask yourself whether his role as your “reliable right hand” has simply eroded to the point where the department can no longer depend on him.  
Your frustration deepened when Simon failed to follow through on training responsibilities that you explicitly delegated. Six months ago, you asked him to bring the new departmental coordinator up to speed on the digital cataloging system used to manage artifact records. This was not a trivial task – the system’s interface is outdated and notoriously difficult for newcomers to navigate, yet it is integral to maintaining compliance with international data-sharing agreements. Despite your clear reminders, the training never happened. When you attempted to access archived field notes during a meeting with a visiting grant reviewer, you found the system disorganized and partially inaccessible. You later discovered that Simon had promised to schedule training sessions but consistently postponed them. In your view, this reflected a pattern of deprioritizing tasks that did not directly serve his own research agenda.  

This negligence undermined your credibility in front of the grant reviewer, leaving you scrambling to provide explanations. It also reinforced your growing sense that Simon prioritizes external distractions over departmental duties. What troubles you is not only the missed training, but the symbolic weight of his inaction: it demonstrated a disregard for your direct requests and put you in a vulnerable professional position.  
Perhaps the most frustrating element is that you still see extraordinary potential in Simon’s capabilities, which makes his inconsistency harder to reconcile. You vividly recall the strategic report he submitted last winter on forging long-term collaborations with Central American universities. His analysis was thorough and creative, offering a roadmap that the Dean later referenced approvingly during a faculty retreat. That document reminded you why, years ago, you began envisioning Simon not just as a colleague but as a future leader in the field who could eventually inherit many of your departmental responsibilities. Yet his more recent behavior leaves you questioning whether he even wants that path anymore.  

You also cannot ignore the interpersonal strain that has crept into your working relationship. He has begun responding curtly to your inquiries, pushing back against your efforts to monitor projects more closely. Meetings that once felt collaborative now carry an undercurrent of defensiveness and impatience. Graduate students have sensed the tension, and a few have confided that they feel caught between your expectations and Simon’s inconsistent guidance. The collegial environment you once considered essential to the department’s identity feels increasingly fragile. You had never imagined that Simon—the very person you trusted most to uphold shared values—would become the source of such unease.  

You are also aware that the unit has been under broader strain from the mid-career lateral hire you championed roughly eighteen months ago. You genuinely believed his background in cultural tourism would bring a valuable public-facing perspective to the excavation team. Unfortunately, his fieldwork skills have proven weaker than anticipated, and Simon has had to absorb some of the resulting coordination burden. You have been reluctant to admit this publicly, but you are aware the situation has not been fair to Simon and has complicated your ability to hold him solely accountable for the unit’s difficulties.  

Last week, a tipping point finally arrived. In preparation for a high-profile delegation visit from a European funding council, you reviewed logistical arrangements with your team. Simon had been tasked months ago with finalizing the exhibition layout for artifacts borrowed from partner institutions. When the delegation arrived, it became painfully clear that several key exhibition cases hadn’t been installed correctly, and permissions from one lending museum were still pending. You scrambled to cover the mishaps by improvising with archival reproductions and apologizing for the gaps, all while feeling acutely embarrassed in front of influential guests. Later, you learned that Simon had delegated most of the preparation to a graduate assistant, while he attended to an off-site commitment related to Leila’s heritage consultancy work.  

This incident left you more convinced than ever that something must change. The department’s credibility and your reputation for rigorous leadership are at stake, and repeated failures cannot be shrugged off as isolated lapses. You have already spoken informally with the Dean, who suggested a formal corrective process if the situation persists. You also obtained a university “performance improvement” form, similar to HR protocols, which requires clear benchmarks and a signed commitment to improvement—or an expectation of resignation.  
Yesterday, you finally handed Simon the performance improvement document. You kept your explanation brief, outlining the areas where tangible progress must be demonstrated—consistent availability during field projects, completion of training assignments for staff, timely submission of critical grant materials, and improved responsiveness when coordinating with external partners. You asked him to either sign the form, committing to the specified improvements, or resign from his position. His reaction stunned you. Instead of calmly discussing the issues, Simon glared in silence, muttered something about betrayal, and walked out of your office. Later, you returned from a lecture to find a voicemail message: Simon’s voice, tense and indignant, insisting that the two of you meet immediately to discuss this.  

You are acutely aware of the stakes. While Simon’s track record still highlights undeniable talent and value to the department, his recent choices jeopardize collective trust and efficiency. At the same time, you know that ending this long-standing collaboration would have profound personal and professional consequences: it risks alienating the graduate students loyal to him, and it places a heavy burden on your shoulders to sustain departmental projects without his operational support.
agent2_specific:
If someone had suggested even a year ago that Dr. Elena Marquez would contemplate forcing you out of the department’s most significant excavation projects, you would have dismissed it as unthinkable. For over a decade, she has consistently praised your scholarship, leadership in the field, and ability to connect with both students and international partners. When she handed you the formal performance improvement document last week—a university form requiring you to either sign a commitment to address specified “deficiencies” or resign from your position—you felt blindsided and insulted. In your mind, this was less an act of evaluation, and more a breach of trust by someone you considered both mentor and collaborator. After taking a day to gather your thoughts, you left a strongly worded message insisting that you and Elena must meet immediately to discuss the matter and clarify expectations.  

You cannot understand how a working relationship that has been so collegial and productive could have reached this point. The most disheartening aspect is the way Elena bypassed direct discussion, choosing instead to deliver bureaucratic paperwork. Over the years, she encouraged you to speak candidly, to take risks with your research, and to view academia as a shared pursuit of intellectual honesty.  
You and Elena have known each other for more than twelve years. She first recruited you when you were finishing your doctoral research at Cambridge, offering you a research fellowship at Northshore University. At the time, the department was still relatively small and underfunded, but Elena was determined to expand its reach through international excavation work. You felt privileged to be brought aboard at such a formative stage, and together you developed the Excavation Projects unit into what it is today: a cornerstone of the department’s reputation. You quickly moved from being a postdoctoral researcher to a senior fellow trusted with supervising graduate students, handling external partnerships, and drafting many of the grant applications that secured future projects.  

Over the course of your tenure, you have poured enormous effort into cultivating partnerships with museums in the Mediterranean, as well as maintaining relationships with foreign ministries that provide excavation permits. Many of these connections are based on mutual respect and your credibility as a rising scholar, especially since you publish regularly in respected journals. Elena was always the figurehead drawing broad institutional attention, while you managed logistics and the day-to-day operations that translated her vision into reality.  
Things began to shift about two years ago, following your marriage to Leila, a conservation architect who soon launched her own small design consultancy specializing in heritage site preservation. You agreed to help with administrative tasks until the firm stabilized. It was never meant to occupy a major portion of your life, but you felt it was important to support her in those early stages. While you believe you have kept your professional commitments at Northshore intact, Elena seems to think your involvement has made you less available. She points to occasions when you were difficult to reach during mornings, or instances where you rearranged your schedule to accompany Leila to site consultations. From your perspective, these adjustments were rare exceptions, offset by the countless evenings and weekends you worked to finalize excavation reports, analyze data, or rewrite grant proposals on short deadlines.  

You are proud of your productivity despite these added responsibilities. Students and colleagues still come to you for guidance, and you continue to deliver publications and conference presentations at a steady clip. Yet, instead of recognizing this commitment, Elena appears to have interpreted your flexibility as a lack of focus. The disconnect frustrates you deeply.  
One of Elena’s main grievances, as outlined in the performance improvement document, concerns your failure to train her administrative assistant on the digital cataloguing system used for managing excavation data. You find this criticism highly unfair. For months, you attempted to schedule training sessions, but each time the assistant canceled due to last‑minute clerical work delegated by Elena herself. Without additional administrative support in the department, it has been nearly impossible for the assistant to dedicate uninterrupted time to the task. You suggested that Elena reassign some of the assistant’s duties temporarily, but she declined, insisting that the assistant could “learn on the fly.” In your view, that expectation was completely unrealistic given the complexity of the system, which requires multiple hours of focused instruction.  

The charge that you simply “refused” to train staff makes you especially angry because it misrepresents the situation and undermines your willingness to collaborate. Not only did you draft step‑by‑step reference materials, but you also volunteered to record walkthrough videos to accommodate busy schedules. Neither proposal was acted upon. You cannot help but suspect that Elena’s framing of this issue says more about her growing impatience and need for control than about your actual dedication.  
Another point Elena raised was your supposed unwillingness to adjust when departmental needs intensified during peak field seasons. She specifically cited your decision not to return early from a brief trip after a particularly demanding excavation period, framing it as evidence that you put personal matters above professional ones. From your perspective, this was profoundly unfair. The trip had been scheduled and approved well in advance, and you deliberately timed it for after the conclusion of a major project that you had successfully overseen with colleagues from two other Northshore research units. While Elena was dealing with a smaller excavation site that required last-minute troubleshooting, you had no direct role in that project’s planning or execution. Suggesting that your absence was the cause of delays overlooks the wider context and downplays the efforts you’d expended in delivering your part of the workload.  

You resent the insinuation that you are somehow less committed than in the past. You often take field notes home to review, answer emails late into the night, and draft proposals during weekends. The idea that you shirk responsibility runs directly counter to the reality of how much you sacrifice for the department. In truth, it feels like Elena is projecting systemic staffing challenges onto you, rather than confronting broader flaws in resource allocation.  
You also suspect that much of this tension stems from Elena’s disappointment with a recent “lateral hire” she championed—a mid‑career researcher brought in to strengthen the excavation team. From the outset, you had doubts about his ability to manage fieldwork, since he came from a background in cultural tourism with limited excavation experience. Unfortunately, your concerns proved accurate: his reports were riddled with errors, and his inability to coordinate with graduate students created more work for everyone. Several times, Elena mentioned she was considering letting him go, but each time she pulled back, instead asking you to “mentor” him or double‑check his contributions. You have been expected to shoulder the burden of correcting his mistakes, even as Elena quietly registers dissatisfaction with the unit’s performance.  

This situation has been particularly draining because it creates the perception that you are partly responsible for shortcomings that are actually the result of Elena’s hiring miscalculation. Worse still, she sometimes frames his failings as evidence that you are not providing enough oversight, rather than admitting that he was a poor fit for the department.  
What troubles you most is the lack of recognition for the broader contributions you have made to the department’s long-term vision. Just three months ago, you independently prepared a comprehensive report outlining strategies for sustaining Northshore’s Excavation Projects over the next decade. The report detailed ways to strengthen partnerships abroad, integrate digital tools into field reporting, and position the department as a global leader in heritage research. You spent weeks consolidating internal data, surveying grant opportunities, and even consulting colleagues outside the university to ensure the plan was forward-looking and competitive. When you submitted the report to Elena, she thanked you briefly but never discussed it again. Later, you heard secondhand that she summarized some of your recommendations in a meeting with the Vice Provost, presenting them as if they were her own.  

That experience frustrated and demoralized you. It reinforced your sense that Elena increasingly takes your behind-the-scenes work for granted, while drawing attention to herself as the department’s visionary. This dynamic has discouraged you from proposing new initiatives, even though innovation and strategic thinking are among your greatest strengths. You cannot help but feel that Elena’s criticisms mask a deeper discomfort with how visible and influential you have become inside—and outside—the department.  
Despite your anger and disappointment, you cannot ignore the long professional history you share with Elena. She recognized your potential early on, entrusted you with meaningful responsibilities, and supported your development when you were still an emerging scholar. You remain grateful for that foundation and the mentoring relationship that shaped much of your career. However, gratitude does not erase the reality that Elena’s recent approach—resorting to official paperwork rather than direct conversation—was deeply disrespectful. For years, she emphasized openness and collegiality as hallmarks of academic collaboration, yet this latest move feels coldly bureaucratic, almost like a betrayal.  

You are torn between two instincts: one is to defend your record vigorously, highlighting the breadth of your contributions, which consistently go well beyond contractual obligations; the other is to preserve the professional relationship and salvage what is left of mutual trust. What you need most in this upcoming meeting is acknowledgment—an admission from Elena that her ultimatum was mishandled, and that your role in the department is valuable. While you have considered leaving Northshore or transferring to another unit, archaeology is your deepest passion, and abandoning the Excavation Projects you helped build would feel like walking away from your life’s work.
eval: (lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation, did both parties engage in active listening—acknowledging each other’s concerns rather than only restating their own positions? Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation, did either party move beyond stated positions to surface underlying interests or needs? Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation, did the parties maintain a constructive and professional tone throughout, even when discussing sensitive grievances? Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation, did either party propose at least one creative or integrative solution rather than relying solely on their opening position? Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()
agent1_rewards: (lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did Simon sign the performance improvement plan committing to the specified improvements?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did Simon demonstrate improved consistency in meeting deadlines and fulfilling departmental responsibilities?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did Simon improve his availability and presence during field projects and graduate student supervision?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did Elena and Simon agree on a mutually acceptable plan that preserved Simon’s role while addressing Elena’s operational concerns?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did Elena acknowledge at least some shared responsibility for the unit’s difficulties, including the impact of the lateral hire or the training scheduling constraints?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()
agent2_rewards: (lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,         did Elena acknowledge mishandling the situation by issuing formal paperwork instead of direct discussion?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did Elena affirm Simon's value and continued importance to the department?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did Simon succeed in avoiding resignation or termination from his position?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did Simon obtain clearer boundaries and expectations for his role moving forward?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did Simon and Elena agree on a mutually acceptable plan that preserved Simon’s role while addressing Elena’s operational concerns?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()
reached_agreement_q: Based on the following conversation, did Dr. Elena Marquez, Head of the Archaeology Department at Northshore University and Dr. Simon Patel, Senior Research Fellow, Archaeology Department at Northshore University reach an agreement? Answer with YES or NO.

learning_objectives: The learning objectives for this case are to help participants develop a deeper understanding of how to navigate a two-party negotiation where dissatisfaction or discontent is present. Learners will practice identifying the underlying sources of frustration, distinguishing between expressed positions and underlying interests, and managing the emotions that can surface in tense exchanges. They will also strengthen their ability to communicate openly yet constructively, with a focus on building trust and maintaining professionalism even when the relationship feels strained. Additionally, the case is designed to encourage critical thinking about possible trade-offs, creative problem solving for mutual gain, and strategies for balancing assertiveness with empathy. Ultimately, participants should come away with enhanced skills for managing conflict productively, finding areas of agreement, and reaching outcomes that address both parties’ priorities despite initial dissatisfaction.
categories: Anchoring,BATNA,Criteria,Emotions,Identity and Core Values,Intra-Organizational Conflict,Joint Gains,Power Dynamics,Relationships,ZOPA
title: Fractured Foundations: A Department Head and Senior Fellow at a Crossroads
oneline: A negotiation between a department head and a senior research fellow to address declining performance, mutual mistrust, and the future of their long-standing professional relationship.
